---
title: "MD7115 Part 1"
subtitle: "Introduction and Setup"
author: '<br><br>Xiaotao Shen <br> Assistant Professor <br> <a href="http://shen-lab.org"> <i class="fa fa-home fa-fw"></i>&nbsp; shen-lab.org</a><br> <a href="mailto:xiaotao.shen@ntu.edu.sg"><i class="fa fa-paper-plane fa-fw"></i>&nbsp; xiaotao.shen@ntu.edu.sg</a><br>'
institute: "Lee Kong Chian School of Medicine, NTU Singapore"
date: '`r Sys.Date()`'
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: [xaringan-themer.css, my-theme.css]
    self_contained: true
    nature:
      ratio: '16:9'
      countdown: false
      titleSlideClass: [center, middle]
      countIncrementalSlides: false
    seal: true
    yolo: false
---

layout: true
background-image: url(images/lkc_logo.png)
background-position: 100% 0%
background-size: 20%

<div class="my-footer"><span>Xiaotao Shen/www.shen-lab.org</span></div>

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  eval = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  cache = FALSE,
  dpi = 72
)
library(tidyverse)
```

# About this session

- Topic: graphs, networks, and trees in biomedical research
- Audience: PhD students with basic biological data analysis experience
- Duration: 3 hours total
- Format: concepts + one main live demo + one tree showcase

---

# Learning goals

By the end of this class, you should be able to:

1. Explain the difference between a graph, a network, and a tree
2. Read node and edge tables into R
3. Build a simple network object and customize a plot
4. Interpret a tree visualization with biological annotations

---

# Course roadmap

1. Introduction and setup
2. What are graph, network, and tree?
3. Network analysis in R
4. Tree visualization in biology
5. Interpretation, pitfalls, and take-home messages

---

# Before class

- Install R and RStudio
- Download or clone the course repository
- Run `1_code/0_install_packages.R`
- Open the project file `MD7115-NTU.Rproj`

---

# Recommended workflow

.pull-left[
- Use an RStudio Project
- Keep data, code, and materials in one folder
- Run code from top to bottom
- Save plots as PDF or PNG
]

.pull-right[
<img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jaspershen-lab/MD7115-NTU/main/3_materials/images/rstudio.png" alt="RStudio" width="80%">
]

---

# Folder structure

```text
MD7115-NTU/
├── 1_code/
├── 2_demo_data/
├── 3_materials/
├── MD7115-NTU.Rproj
└── README.md
```

- `1_code/`: runnable examples
- `2_demo_data/`: local datasets for class
- `3_materials/`: slides and figures

---

# Package check

Run this once before class:

```{r}
source("1_code/0_install_packages.R")
```

Load the main packages:

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)
library(phyloseq)
library(ggtree)
```

---

# Data we will use today

- `2_demo_data/example_node_data.csv`
- `2_demo_data/example_edge_data.csv`
- `2_demo_data/pathway_result.csv`
- `2_demo_data/personalized_score.csv`
- `2_demo_data/microbiome_data.RData`

---

# What I want you to focus on

- What does a node mean?
- What does an edge mean?
- What does the layout change?
- What does the visualization help us interpret?

Code syntax matters, but the biological meaning matters more.

---

# Setup checklist

Before we start Part 2, you should have:

- opened the project
- confirmed packages are installed
- located the demo data
- run one or two simple `library()` commands without error

---

# Transition

Next, we move from software setup to concepts.

Question to keep in mind:

**When should we use a graph, a network, or a tree in biomedical research?**
